questions.question: Order the seven steps of the literature-review process.
questions.explanation: The seven steps in order are: search, evaluate, read and note, organise, outline, storyline, write.

questions.question: Order the topic-narrowing funnel from broadest to a single study.
items: Pick one specific study you can run
Choose a broad area of interest
Identify a sub-topic within the area
correct_order: Choose a broad area of interest
Identify a sub-topic within the area
Pick one specific study you can run
questions.explanation: You narrow a broad topic to a sub-topic, then a focused angle, until you can pick one feasible study.

questions.question: Order these steps for handling a definition you want to cite, ending with citing correctly.
items: Notice a secondary source uses the definition
Trace it back to the original source
Confirm the original actually defines the term
correct_order: Notice a secondary source uses the definition
Trace it back to the original source
Confirm the original actually defines the term
questions.explanation: To avoid citing the citation, trace a definition back to the original source, confirm it, and cite that original.

questions.question: Order these steps for formulating a research question from a phenomenon.
items: Check the question is feasible to study
correct_order: Check the question is feasible to study
questions.explanation: From a real phenomenon you identify measurable variables, phrase a specific answerable RQ, then check feasibility, as with Cherry's cocktail-party effect.

questions.question: Order these steps for evaluating a paper before committing to a deep read.
questions.explanation: Pre-reading evaluation scans the abstract and conclusions, weighs citation count and recency, then decides whether a full read is worthwhile.
